32: A fulfillment: 'And it shall come to pass that everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved.' Peter (Acts 2:21) and Paul (Romans 10:13) both cite Joel 2:32 as fulfilled in the gospel proclamation: the 'name of the LORD' whose invocation brings salvation is now identified as Jesus, whom God has made both Lord and Christ (Acts 2:36). The typological identification is clear: YHWH's name in the OT = Jesus's name in the NT. This is not a revision of the OT but the NT's claim that YHWH's name and Jesus's name are the same divine identity now disclosed fully in the incarnation. The universal accessibility of salvation ('everyone who calls') removes the national and ethnic boundaries that had characterized the OT covenant community.
1: A direct revelation: 'Blow the trumpet in Zion; sound the alarm on my holy mountain!' The shofar at Zion signals both alarm and assembly. Matthew 24:31: he will send out his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds. 1 Corinthians 15:52: at the last trumpet ... the dead will be raised imperishable. Revelation 11:15: at the sound of the seventh trumpet the kingdom of the world becomes the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ. Joel's trumpet-blast at Zion is the OT template for the eschatological trumpet that announces the arrival of the Son of Man — the alarm that wakes the dead and gathers the living.
2: A shadow: 'A day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness. Like the dawn spreading over the mountains a great and powerful people.' The darkness imagery of the Day of the LORD appears at the crucifixion: Mark 15:33 — at the sixth hour darkness came over the whole land until the ninth hour. The cross is the Day of the LORD in concentrated form — the wrath that was to come fell on the Son. The darkness at noon was not eclipse but divine sign: the day of cosmic judgment had arrived, not in its final eschatological form but in its redemptive concentrated form, as the Lord himself bore the darkness for those who would have faced it finally.
3: A shadow: 'Before them a fire devours, and behind them a flame burns. Before them the land is like the garden of Eden, and behind them a desolate wilderness.' The reversal of Eden — the garden destroyed by the divine army's advance — reflects the trajectory of the fall and the trajectory of redemption. Behind the army of judgment is desolation; but ahead of the new creation restored by Christ is the garden again: Revelation 22:1–2 (the river of the water of life ... the tree of life ... for the healing of the nations). The fire that leaves Eden-waste behind it is the same fire that Christ came to cast on the earth (Luke 12:49); both fires serve the ultimate restoration of the garden.
4: A shadow: 'Their appearance is like that of horses; and like war horses, so they charge.' Revelation 9:7 directly borrows this image: the locusts from the abyss were like horses prepared for battle. Revelation 19:11 inverts it: the Rider on the white horse leads the armies of heaven — Christ at his parousia commands the army that Joel's locust-imagery had previewed. The locust-horses of Joel 2 are the type of the judgment armies; the white-horse Rider is the one who both leads the final judgment and has already absorbed its central blow at the cross.
5: A shadow: 'Like the rumbling of chariots they leap over the mountaintops, like the crackling of a flame of fire consuming stubble.' Revelation 9:9 (the sound of their wings was like the sound of many chariots with horses rushing into battle) is a direct citation. The unstoppable sound-advance of the divine army is the type of the unstoppable mission of the gospel: Romans 10:18 (their voice has gone out to all the earth, and their words to the ends of the world). The chariots of judgment become the chariots of proclamation when the Judge becomes the Savior.
6: A shadow: 'Before them peoples writhe in anguish; every face turns pale.' Revelation 6:15–17: the kings of the earth and the great ones and the generals and the rich and the powerful hid themselves in the caves and among the rocks of the mountains, calling to the mountains, 'Fall on us and hide us from the face of him who is seated on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb.' The paleness of Joel's peoples before the approaching army is the OT preview of the terror of the Lamb's wrath in Revelation — the same divine face that the covenant people welcome is the face before which the unrepentant flee.
7: A shadow: 'Like warriors they run; like soldiers they scale the wall. Each one keeps to his own course; they do not swerve from their paths.' The disciplined advance of the divine army — each in its assigned course, nothing deviating — is the type of the irresistible completion of the divine plan. God's purposes do not deviate or fail: Isaiah 46:10–11 (I will accomplish all my purpose); John 19:30 (it is finished — tetelestai, the complete execution of the plan). The divine army that keeps its course perfectly is the shadow of the Son who kept his course perfectly from Bethlehem to the empty tomb.
8: A shadow: 'They do not push each other; each advances in his own track. They burst through the weapons without halting; they do not swerve from their paths.' The weapons-resistant advance of the locust army is the type of the unstoppable progress of Christ's mission. John 10:18: no one takes my life from me, but I lay it down of my own accord. The cross was not an interruption of the divine plan but its execution: not the weapons of the enemy stopping God's advance but Christ willingly walking into them. Luke 9:51: he set his face to go to Jerusalem — the same resolute advance that Joel's army models.
9: A shadow: 'They rush through the city; they run along the walls; they climb up into the houses; they enter through the windows like a thief.' The comprehensive penetration of the city — no hiding place, no sealed room — is the type of the inescapable reach of divine judgment. Revelation 3:20: behold, I stand at the door and knock — the same entry-through-every-opening, but now as the invitation of grace rather than the enforcement of judgment. For those who welcome the entry: Revelation 3:20 (I will come in to him and eat with him). For those who resist: the inescapable nature of the final reckoning (Revelation 20:12: the dead were judged by what was written in the books).
10: A direct revelation: 'Before them the earth quakes, the heavens tremble; the sun and the moon grow dark, and the stars withdraw their shining.' The cosmic shaking at the approach of the divine army is cited in Hebrews 12:26–27: at that time his voice shook the earth, but now he has promised, 'Yet once more I will shake not only the earth but also the heavens.' This means the removal of things that are shaken in order that the things that cannot be shaken may remain. Christ's parousia will be this final, comprehensive cosmic shaking — the undoing of the old creation's framework to make room for the new. The dark sun and moon appear at the cross (Mark 15:33) as the preview shaking.
11: A direct revelation: 'The LORD utters his voice before his army, for his forces are exceedingly great; he who executes his word is powerful.' The LORD speaking before his army is the type of the creative-commanding Word who leads the final judgment. Revelation 19:15: from his mouth comes a sharp sword with which to strike down the nations. The word of Christ that once said 'Let there be light' and 'Lazarus, come out' is the same word that will one day say 'Depart' and 'Come, blessed of my Father' (Matthew 25:34, 41). Joel's LORD commanding his army is the God who in Christ exercises this command with both mercy and judgment.
12: A direct revelation: 'Yet even now, declares the LORD, return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping, and with mourning.' The invitation in the very midst of announced judgment — 'yet even now' (vegam attah) — is the grace-logic of the entire gospel. Matthew 11:28: come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Luke 15:20: while he was still a long way off, his father saw him. The 'yet even now' of Joel is the permanent posture of Christ toward the returning: not 'it is too late' but 'even now, return.' 2 Peter 3:9: the Lord is patient, not wishing that any should perish but that all should reach repentance.
13: A direct revelation: 'Rend your hearts and not your garments. Return to the LORD your God, for he is gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love, and he relents over disaster.' This verse contains the self-description of YHWH from Exodus 34:6–7 — the great covenant formula. Christ is the embodiment of this character: John 1:14 (the Word became flesh, full of grace and truth — the NT equivalent of chesed ve-emet). The demand for inward rather than external religion (rend your hearts, not garments) is Jesus's consistent teaching (Matthew 5:3–8; Joel 23:27–28). The God who relents over disaster does not relent arbitrarily but because Christ has absorbed the disaster in his body on the tree.
14: A shadow: 'Who knows? He may turn and relent and leave behind a blessing — a grain offering and a drink offering for the LORD your God.' The 'who knows?' of Joel's penitential appeal is the OT's honest acknowledgment that mercy is not owed but hoped for. 2 Peter 3:9: the Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish. In Christ, the 'who knows?' is answered: it is YHWH's revealed will that all should come to repentance. The grain offering and drink offering that might be left behind — bread and wine — are the very elements Christ designated as the permanent memorial of his body and blood (1 Corinthians 11:25).
15: A type: 'Blow the trumpet in Zion; consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly.' The second trumpet-call in Joel 2 (cf. v1) shifts from alarm to assembly. The assembled community before God is the type of the church gathered in Christ's name. Matthew 18:20: where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them. The solemn assembly of Joel that calls the people to repentance prefigures the NT assembly (ekklesia) that gathers in the name of the risen Christ. The fast that Joel calls finds its NT fulfillment in the fasting that Christ anticipated his disciples would do after his departure (Matthew 9:15: the days will come when the bridegroom is taken from them, and then they will fast).
16: A type: 'Gather the people; consecrate the congregation; assemble the elders; gather the children and nursing infants. Let the bridegroom leave his room, and the bride her chamber.' The inclusive scope of the assembly — elders to nursing infants — prefigures the new covenant's comprehensive invitation. Matthew 19:14: let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven. The bridegroom leaving his chamber at the assembly's most urgent moment is the type of Christ's parousia: the Bridegroom who is coming (Matthew 25:6: 'the bridegroom is coming!') leaves the heavenly chamber to meet his bride.
17: A type: 'Between the vestibule and the altar let the priests weep and say, Spare your people, O LORD, and make not your heritage a reproach.' The priests standing between the vestibule and the altar — the innermost accessible space of the temple — are the type of Christ's high-priestly intercession. Hebrews 7:25: he always lives to make intercession for them. Hebrews 9:24: Christ has entered into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf. The priestly prayer 'spare your people' is what Christ permanently offers in the heavenly sanctuary on behalf of those who are in him. The prayer of Joel's priests became Christ's permanent intercession.
18: A direct revelation: 'Then the LORD became zealous for his land and had compassion on his people.' The divine jealousy/zeal (qanna) for his land and his people is the covenantal passion that drives the entire redemptive mission. John 2:17: his disciples remembered that it is written, 'Zeal for your house will consume me.' Christ's cleansing of the temple is the acting-out of YHWH's zealous love for his dwelling place and his people. The compassion that follows the assembly and fasting of vv12–17 is the template for the Father's response to the returning prodigal (Luke 15:20: his father saw him ... and ran and embraced him and kissed him).
19: A direct revelation: 'The LORD answered and said to his people: Behold, I am sending you grain, wine, and oil, and you will be satisfied.' The divine response to the penitential assembly: provision of grain, wine, and oil. These three elements — the staples of the covenant land — are also the elements of the Eucharist in symbolic form: grain (bread) and wine as Christ's body and blood (1 Corinthians 11:24–25), and oil as the anointing of the Spirit (Acts 10:38: God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit). The restoration of what the locust took is also the provision of the three liturgical elements that Christ designates as the memorial of his sacrifice.
20: A shadow: 'I will drive the northern invader far from you, into a parched and desolate land.' The expulsion of the adversarial northern army into permanent desolation is the type of the final defeat of the adversary. Revelation 20:10: the devil who had deceived them was thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur. The northern invader whose stench rises to heaven is destroyed permanently — not just pushed back temporarily but consigned to desolation. Christ's victory at the cross (Colossians 2:15: he disarmed the rulers and authorities) begins this expulsion that is consummated at the parousia.
21: A direct revelation: 'Fear not, O land; be glad and rejoice, for the LORD has done great things.' The fear-not oracle following the defeat of the locust-army is the Easter proclamation in miniature. Luke 1:49: he who is mighty has done great things for me (megaleia). Acts 2:11: we hear them telling in our own tongues the mighty works of God (megalia tou Theou). The angel's announcement to the women at the tomb: Do not be afraid (Matthew 28:5). The structure is Joel's: (1) judgment comes; (2) assembly and repentance; (3) YHWH acts; (4) fear not, great things have been done. The resurrection is the supreme great thing YHWH has done, fulfilling the pattern Joel established.
22: A shadow: 'Fear not, you wild animals, for the pastures of the wilderness are green again; the trees are bearing their fruit; the fig tree and vine give their full yield.' The restoration of creation — even the wild animals reassured — is the beginning of what Romans 8:21 calls the 'freedom of the glory of the children of God' into which the whole creation will be released. The fear of the animals in a barren creation (v18) gives way to a flourishing in which animal life participates. Isaiah 11:6–9 and Rev 21–22 extend this vision: the new creation's fruitfulness encompasses not just human redemption but the restoration of the entire natural order.
23: A direct revelation: 'Be glad, O children of Zion, and rejoice in the LORD your God, for he has given the early rain for your vindication (moreh litzedaqah).' The Hebrew moreh litzedaqah can mean both 'early rain for righteousness' (agricultural reading) and 'teacher of righteousness' (sapiential reading). The early rain in late autumn (October–November) is the soil-softening rain that makes planting possible; the latter rain (April–May) fills the grain before harvest. As 'teacher of righteousness,' the phrase was read mesianically in Dead Sea Scrolls texts. Whether or not Joel intends the messianic reading, Christ as the one who gives both the Spirit (the rain of the new covenant) and is himself the Teacher of righteousness (John 13:13–14) fulfills both senses.
24: A direct revelation: 'The threshing floors shall be full of grain; the vats shall overflow with wine and oil.' The overflowing abundance — threshing floors and vats of wine and oil — is the eschatological harvest imagery that runs through both Testaments. Revelation 14:14–16: the Son of Man sits on a white cloud with a golden crown, and an angel cries 'Put in your sickle, and reap, for the hour to reap has come, for the harvest of the earth is fully ripe.' The grain and wine that overflow Joel's restored land are the same grain and wine that Jesus designated at the Last Supper as his body and blood — the harvest of his own life poured out for many (Mark 14:24).
25: A direct revelation: 'I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten — the hopping locust, the stripping locust, and the cutting locust, my great army which I sent among you.' The restoration of what was consumed — the years themselves — is the OT's most radical promise of redemption: not just recovery of property but recovery of time. Ephesians 5:16: making the best use of the time (exagorazomenoi ton kairon, redeeming the time). Christ's redemption reaches backward to restore what was lost: the resurrection ensures that nothing good is ultimately lost. Revelation 21:4–5: he will wipe away every tear from their eyes ... behold, I am making all things new — including the years the locust ate.
26: A direct revelation: 'You shall eat in plenty and be satisfied, and praise the name of the LORD your God, who has dealt wondrously with you.' The eating to satisfaction and praising is the description of the messianic feast. Revelation 19:9: blessed are those who are invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb. The praise of YHWH's name follows from the great deeds done for his people: Luke 1:49 (Mary's Magnificat: he who is mighty has done great things for me, and holy is his name). The satisfaction of Joel 2:26 is the satisfaction Christ offers (John 6:35: whoever comes to me shall not hunger) — permanent, not subject to the locust's return.
27: A direct revelation: 'You shall know that I am in the midst of Israel, and that I am the LORD your God and there is none else.' The divine presence in the midst of Israel is the covenant promise (Exodus 29:45: I will dwell among the people of Israel and will be their God). John 14:20: in that day you will know that I am in my Father, and you in me, and I in you. Matthew 1:23: Emmanuel — God with us. The knowledge that YHWH is in the midst of his people is fulfilled first in the incarnation (God bodily present among his people), then through the Spirit (John 14:17: the Spirit of truth ... dwells with you and will be in you), and finally in the new creation (Revelation 21:3: the dwelling of God is with man).
28: A direct fulfillment: 'And it shall come to pass afterward, that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions.' Peter explicitly cites this as fulfilled at Pentecost (Acts 2:16–17: this is what was uttered through the prophet Joel). The universal Spirit-gift breaks down the structures that had limited prophetic access: not just prophets and kings but sons and daughters, old and young, male and female servants (v29). Christ is the baptizer with the Holy Spirit (Matthew 3:11: he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire) — the outpouring of Joel 2:28 occurs because Christ has been exalted and poured out the Spirit (Acts 2:33).
29: A direct fulfillment: 'Even on the male and female servants in those days I will pour out my Spirit.' The extension of the Spirit to slaves is the social revolution of the new covenant. Galatians 3:28: there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus. Acts 2:18: even on my male servants and female servants in those days I will pour out my Spirit. The social hierarchy that excluded servants from the prophetic vocation is dissolved by the Spirit's universal outpouring — a direct consequence of Christ's exaltation: Acts 2:33 (being therefore exalted at the right hand of God, and having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing).
30: A shadow: 'And I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth: blood and fire and columns of smoke.' The cosmic signs — blood, fire, smoke — appeared in concentrated form at the crucifixion: darkness over the land (Mark 15:33), the veil torn (Mark 15:38), the earthquake (Matthew 27:51). These are the inauguration of the Day-of-the-LORD signs that Joel announces. The final fulfillment is the parousia: Revelation 8:5–7 (thunder, lightning, earthquake, hail and fire mixed with blood). Every sign Joel announces previews either the cross or the parousia — Christ's two advents are the two poles of the Day-of-the-LORD's fulfillment.
31: A direct revelation: 'The sun shall be turned to darkness, and the moon to blood, before the great and awesome day of the LORD comes.' Jesus cites this directly (Matthew 24:29: immediately after the tribulation of those days the sun will be darkened and the moon will not give its light; Mark 13:24). Revelation 6:12: when he opened the sixth seal, the sun became black as sackcloth and the full moon became like blood. Peter at Pentecost (Acts 2:20) cites this verse as part of the Joel 2:28–32 prophecy being fulfilled — the cosmic signs are the eschatological frame within which the Spirit's outpouring takes place. The sun-to-darkness at the crucifixion (Mark 15:33) is the historical pre-fulfillment; the parousia-darkness is the final fulfillment.
